The Effects of Temperature, Vapor Pressure and Absolute Humidity on Bacterial Contamination of Shell Eggs
papers.abstract: Abstract BACTERIAL contamination of shell eggs is one of the major problems of the poultry industry. A knowledge of variable conditions as related to this contamination would aid in the solution of the problem. Hadley and Caldwell (1916) found fresh eggs to be free from the bacteria chiefly concerned with the decomposition of eggs. The shell has also been found to be sterile in eggs removed from the oviduct or handled under aseptic conditions when laid (Stuart and McNally, 1943). If the shell and contents of the egg are sterile at oviposition, the problem of egg spoilage must be concerned with eggs after they are laid. McNally (1954) stored eggs under controlled conditions of temperature and humidity for from one to three weeks and reported that bacterial infection varied directly with temperature and relative humidity. As part of the trial he stored some eggs with damp shells and some with dry …

papers.title: Heart Probe Holding Board
papers.abstract: Abstract In the past numerous workers have obtained blood samples by the heart probe method, originally described by Sloan and Wilgus (1930) and improved by Andrews (1944). Sloan and Wilgus (1930) mentioned the necessity of stretching and holding the bird immobile for ease in locating the heart. This note describes construction of a board for holding the bird completely immobile during the procedure of probing the heart for blood samples. The holding board (Figure 1) is easily constructed, requiring a minimum of materials. The main board is ½ inch or more thick and measures 12×26 inches. It is supported by three 2×12 inch cleats, ¾ inch thick. On the top edge, 5½ inches from the left corner, is a 1 inch notch 2 inches deep, angled toward the lower right corner of the board. In the lower right corner, 3 inches from the end and 1¾ inches above the lower edge, …
